From `plot_bar.py` in the SP lecture notes, we have:

```python
X = np.arange(n)
rng = np.random.default_rng()
Y1 = (1 - X / float(n)) * rng.uniform(0.5, 1.0, n)

for x, y in zip(X, Y1):
    plt.text(x, y + 0.05, f"{y:.2f}", ha="center", va="bottom")
```

Mypy reports typing errors:

```
intro/matplotlib/examples/plot_bar.py:22: error: Argument 1 to "text" has incompatible type "signedinteger[Any]"; expected "float"  [arg-type]
intro/matplotlib/examples/plot_bar.py:22: error: Argument 2 to "text" has incompatible type "floating[Any] | float64"; expected "float"  [arg-type]
intro/matplotlib/examples/plot_bar.py:25: error: Argument 1 to "text" has incompatible type "signedinteger[Any]"; expected "float"  [arg-type]
intro/matplotlib/examples/plot_bar.py:25: error: Argument 2 to "text" has incompatible type "floating[Any] | float64"; expected "float"  [arg-type]
```

### Proposed solution


Could we broaden the floating point & integer types accepted?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29332/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29332/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/comments/2547804039","html_url":"https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29332#issuecomment-2547804039","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29332","id":2547804039,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABUios6X3GeH","user":{"login":"stefanv","id":45071,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ1MDcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/45071?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv","html_url":"https://github.com/stefanv","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-17T08:33:50Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T08:33:50Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I could make a separate issue for this, but same principle:

```
  /home/stefan/p/scientific-python/lectures/intro/matplotlib/examples/plot_plot.py:21:32 - error: Argument of type "NDArray[numpy.bool[builtins.bool]]" cannot be assigned to parameter "where" of type "Sequence[bool] | None"
```

I believe we already discussed, elsewhere, the extents keywords that only accept tuples.

Describe the issue:

The following code seems correct, and passes type checking under 2.1.3 but not 2.2.0:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(27446968)
n_states = 5

P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)

p = P.T @ p
```

I suppose mypy is flagging that the float width can increase, but since float64 is used by random, that won't happen in this scenario.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
N/A
```


### Error message:

```shell
foo.py:10: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating[Any]]]", variable has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]")  [assignment]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.13, mypy 1.13, numpy 2.2.0

Description

Fixes https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7636.

This was deprecated in matplotlib 3.8. I didn't really figure out an equivalent method that can use `set_label`. I usually got a "Legend does not support handles for QuadContourSet" error. So instead, I used proxy artists as suggested by
https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/explain/axes/legend_guide.html#creating-artists-specifically-for-adding-to-the-legend-aka-proxy-artists

Also update the API to use "import skimage as ski".

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

Description

Fixes https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7636.

This was deprecated in matplotlib 3.8. I didn't really figure out an equivalent method that can use `set_label`. I usually got a "Legend does not support handles for QuadContourSet" error. So instead, I used proxy artists as suggested by
https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/explain/axes/legend_guide.html#creating-artists-specifically-for-adding-to-the-legend-aka-proxy-artists

Also update the API to use "import skimage as ski".

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

Description

Fixes https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7636.

This was deprecated in matplotlib 3.8. I didn't really figure out an equivalent method that can use `set_label`. I usually got a "Legend does not support handles for QuadContourSet" error. So instead, I used proxy artists as suggested by
https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/explain/axes/legend_guide.html#creating-artists-specifically-for-adding-to-the-legend-aka-proxy-artists

Also update the API to use "import skimage as ski".

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

Description

Fixes https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7636.

This was deprecated in matplotlib 3.8. I didn't really figure out an equivalent method that can use `set_label`. I usually got a "Legend does not support handles for QuadContourSet" error. So instead, I used proxy artists as suggested by
https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/explain/axes/legend_guide.html#creating-artists-specifically-for-adding-to-the-legend-aka-proxy-artists

Also update the API to use "import skimage as ski".

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

Describe the issue:

The following code seems correct, and passes type checking under 2.1.3 but not 2.2.0:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(27446968)
n_states = 5

P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)

p = P.T @ p
```

I suppose mypy is flagging that the float width can increase, but since float64 is used by random, that won't happen in this scenario.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
N/A
```


### Error message:

```shell
foo.py:10: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating[Any]]]", variable has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]")  [assignment]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.13, mypy 1.13, numpy 2.2.0

Describe the issue:

The following code seems correct, and passes type checking under 2.1.3 but not 2.2.0:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(27446968)
n_states = 5

P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)

p = P.T @ p
```

I suppose mypy is flagging that the float width can increase, but since float64 is used by random, that won't happen in this scenario.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
N/A
```


### Error message:

```shell
foo.py:10: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating[Any]]]", variable has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]")  [assignment]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.13, mypy 1.13, numpy 2.2.0

When trying to build the Debian package for version 0.25.0, I get the failures of **test_2d_bf**, **test_2d_cg**, **test_2d_cg_mg** and **test_2d_cg_j** with the **float16** parameter:
```

dtype = <class 'numpy.float16'>

    def test_2d_bf(dtype):
        lx = 70
        ly = 100
    
        # have to use a smaller beta to avoid warning with lower precision input
        beta = 90 if dtype == np.float64 else 25
    
        data, labels = make_2d_syntheticdata(lx, ly)

>       labels_bf = random_walker(data, labels, beta=beta, mode='bf')

### Way to reproduce:

This happens when running the tests during the Debian build of the package.

### Version information:

* Python 3.12.8 and Python 3.13.1
* Linux-6.11.10-amd64-x86_64-with-glibc2.40
* skimage 0.25.0
* numpy 1.26.4
* scipy 1.14.1

Description

Fixes https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7636.

This was deprecated in matplotlib 3.8. I didn't really figure out an equivalent method that can use `set_label`. I usually got a "Legend does not support handles for QuadContourSet" error. So instead, I used proxy artists as suggested by
https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/explain/axes/legend_guide.html#creating-artists-specifically-for-adding-to-the-legend-aka-proxy-artists

Also update the API to use "import skimage as ski".

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

Description

Fixes https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7636.

This was deprecated in matplotlib 3.8. I didn't really figure out an equivalent method that can use `set_label`. I usually got a "Legend does not support handles for QuadContourSet" error. So instead, I used proxy artists as suggested by
https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/explain/axes/legend_guide.html#creating-artists-specifically-for-adding-to-the-legend-aka-proxy-artists

Also update the API to use "import skimage as ski".

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

Problem

In the scikit-image gallery, we sometimes draw several contours, and then want to label them.
These contours are all based on processed iterations of a dataset, so cannot be drawn with a single `contour` call.

We have a [workarounds for getting those contours labeled](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/7638#pullrequestreview-2509633501), after API changes in 3.10, but it would be really nice if we could simply join the contoursets, and then use `plt.clabel` to do the job.

(By the way, contoursets confusingly have members such as `set_label` and `extend`, presumably inherited, which don't do what you'd expect; would perhaps be good to hide them?)

### Proposed solution

Allow contoursets to be joined as regular sets.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29339/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":1},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29339/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/comments/2549445135","html_url":"https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29339#issuecomment-2549445135","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/29339","id":2549445135,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABUios6X9XIP","user":{"login":"stefanv","id":45071,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ1MDcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/45071?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv","html_url":"https://github.com/stefanv","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-17T19:36:52Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T19:36:52Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> I'm not really following your use case = the workaround linked is to legend entries, whereas here you are talking about `label`. l":"https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/7638.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"## Description

Fixes https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/7636.

This was deprecated in matplotlib 3.8. I didn't really figure out an equivalent method that can use `set_label`. I usually got a "Legend does not support handles for QuadContourSet" error. So instead, I used proxy artists as suggested by
https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/explain/axes/legend_guide.html#creating-artists-specifically-for-adding-to-the-legend-aka-proxy-artists

Also update the API to use "import skimage as ski".

## Checklist

<!-- Before pull requests can be merged, they should provide: -->

- A descriptive but concise pull request title
- [Docstrings for all functions](https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/blob/main/doc/example.py)
- [Unit tests](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html#testing)
- A gallery example in `./doc/examples` for new features
- [Contribution guide](https://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/development/contribute.html) is followed

## Release note

For maintainers and optionally contributors, please refer to the [instructions](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/development/contribute.html#documenting-changes) on how to document this PR for the release notes.

```release-note
...
```

`validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. `validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. First time pull requesting to a public repo, let me know if anything is `validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. First time pull requesting to a public repo, let me know if anything is `validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. First time pull requesting to a public repo, let me know if anything is `validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. First time pull requesting to a public repo, let me know if anything is Description:

When trying to build the Debian package for version 0.25.0, I get the failures of **test_2d_bf**, **test_2d_cg**, **test_2d_cg_mg** and **test_2d_cg_j** with the **float16** parameter:
```

dtype = <class 'numpy.float16'>

    @testing.parametrize('dtype', [np.float16, np.float32, np.float64])
    def test_2d_bf(dtype):
        lx = 70
        ly = 100
    
        # have to use a smaller beta to avoid warning with lower precision input
        beta = 90 if dtype == np.float64 else 25
    
        data, labels = make_2d_syntheticdata(lx, ly)
        data = data.astype(dtype, copy=False)
>       labels_bf = random_walker(data, labels, beta=beta, mode='bf')

skimage/segmentation/tests/test_random_walker.py:73: 
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ 
skimage/_shared/utils.py:445: in fixed_func
    return func(*args, **kwargs)
skimage/segmentation/random_walker_segmentation.py:554: in random_walker
    lap_sparse, B = _build_linear_system(
skimage/segmentation/random_walker_segmentation.py:168: in _build_linear_system
    lap_sparse = _build_laplacian(
skimage/segmentation/random_walker_segmentation.py:148: in _build_laplacian
    lap = sparse.csr_array((data, (i_indices, j_indices)), shape=(pixel_nb, pixel_nb))
/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/scipy/sparse/_compressed.py:56: in __init__
    arrays = coo._coo_to_compressed(self._swap)
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ 

self = <COOrdinate sparse array of dtype 'float16'
        with 27660 stored elements and shape (7000, 7000)>
swap = <function _csr_base._swap at 0x7f737c6fbce0>, copy = False
    def _coo_to_compressed(self, swap, copy=False):
        """convert (shape, coords, data) to (indptr, indices, data, shape)"""
        M, N = swap(self._shape_as_2d)
        # convert idx_dtype intc to int32 for pythran.
        # tested in scipy/optimize/tests/test__numdiff.py::test_group_columns
        idx_dtype = self._get_index_dtype(self.coords, maxval=max(self.nnz, N))
    
        if self.ndim == 1:
            indices = self.coords[0].copy() if copy else self.coords[0]
            nnz = len(indices)
            indptr = np.array([0, nnz], dtype=idx_dtype)
            data = self.data.copy() if copy else self.data
            return indptr, indices, data, self.shape
    
        # ndim == 2
        major, minor = swap(self.coords)
        nnz = len(major)
        major = major.astype(idx_dtype, copy=False)
        minor = minor.astype(idx_dtype, copy=False)
    
        indptr = np.empty(M + 1, dtype=idx_dtype)
        indices = np.empty_like(minor, dtype=idx_dtype)
        data = np.empty_like(self.data, dtype=self.dtype)
    
>       coo_tocsr(M, N, nnz, major, minor, self.data, indptr, indices, data)
E       ValueError: Output dtype not compatible with inputs.

/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/scipy/sparse/_coo.py:374: ValueError
```

### Way to reproduce:

This happens when running the tests during the Debian build of the package.

### Version information:

```Shell
* Python 3.12.8 and Python 3.13.1
* Linux-6.11.10-amd64-x86_64-with-glibc2.40
* skimage 0.25.0
* numpy 1.26.4
* scipy 1.14.1
```

Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Hi all! This PR adds a "Translations team" section to the teams page. The toml file is manually added since translators are members of crowdin but not necessarily github.

I am happy to add links to github profiles and/or new avatars, just let me know. As a placeholder, folks without an avatar are showing up as the NumPy logo.

Closes #796

The return type for `add_subplots` with `projection="3d"` should be `Axes3D`.

The current implementation is:

```python
    @overload
    def add_subplot(
        self, nrows: int, ncols: int, index: int | tuple[int, int], **kwargs
    ) -> Axes: ...
    @overload
    def add_subplot(self, pos: int, **kwargs) -> Axes: ...
    @overload
    def add_subplot(self, ax: Axes, **kwargs) -> Axes: ...
    @overload
    def add_subplot(self, ax: SubplotSpec, **kwargs) -> Axes: ...
    @overload
    def add_subplot(self, **kwargs) -> Axes: ...
    @overload
    def subplots(
```

### Code for reproduction

```Python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.axes3d import Axes3D
ax: Axes3D = plt.figure().add_subplot(projection="3d")
```


### Actual outcome

Pyright complains:

```
error: Type "Axes" is not assignable to declared type "Axes3D"
    "Axes" is not assignable to "Axes3D" (reportAssignmentType)
```

### Expected outcome

No error.

`validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. `validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. First time pull requesting to a public repo, let me know if anything is `validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. First time pull requesting to a public repo, let me know if anything is `validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. First time pull requesting to a public repo, let me know if anything is `validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. `validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. First time pull requesting to a public repo, let me know if anything is Describe the issue:

The following code seems correct, and passes type checking under 2.1.3 but not 2.2.0:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(27446968)
n_states = 5

P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)

p = P.T @ p
```

I suppose mypy is flagging that the float width can increase, but since float64 is used by random, that won't happen in this scenario.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
N/A
```


### Error message:

```shell
foo.py:10: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating[Any]]]", variable has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]")  [assignment]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.13, mypy 1.13, numpy 2.2.0

Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Hi all! This PR adds a "Translations team" section to the teams page. The toml file is manually added since translators are members of crowdin but not necessarily github.

I am happy to add links to github profiles and/or new avatars, just let me know. As a placeholder, folks without an avatar are showing up as the NumPy logo.

Closes #796

I'd like to propose using the Teams gallery page provided by the scientific python hugo theme to show a "Translation Team".

The only issue is that right now the Teams page is sourced directly from GitHub teams, and there are two problems there:
- There is no translation team on github (but this would be easily fixed)
- Not every translator has a github account - they log in to Crowdin with potentially other credentials.

If there is interest and consensus, could we have a manual section on the teams page to add translators? Or source them directly from crowdin?

cc @steppi ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/796/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy.org/issues/796/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Hi all! This PR adds a "Translations team" section to the teams page. The toml file is manually added since translators are members of crowdin but not necessarily github.

I am happy to add links to github profiles and/or new avatars, just let me know. As a placeholder, folks without an avatar are showing up as the NumPy logo.

Closes #796

 ---
 
-Installations methods include:
+The recommended method of installing SciPy depends on your preferred workflow.
+The common workflows can roughly be broken down into the following
+categories:
 
-- [Distributions](#distributions)
-- [pip](#pip-install)
-- [conda](#pip-install)
-- [Package Manager](#package_manager)
-- [Source](#source)
+- [Environment-based (e.g. `pip`, `conda`)](#environment-based) (the traditional workflow)

If you want to be more nuanced about it, you could say, for env based: "recommended for beginners", and for project-based: "recommended for advanced {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/pulls/comments/1890598592","pull_request_review_id":2512432349,"id":1890598592,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIq85wsD7A","diff_hunk":"@@ -3,101 +3,149 @@ title: Installation sidebar: false --- -Installations methods include: +The recommended method of installing SciPy depends on your preferred workflow. +The common workflows can roughly be broken down into the following +categories: -- [Distributions](#distributions) -- [pip](#pip-install) -- [conda](#pip-install) -- [Package Manager](#package_manager) -- [Source](#source) +- [Environment-based (e.g. `pip`, `conda`)](#environment-based) (the traditional workflow) +- [Project-based (e.g. `uv`, `pixi`)](#project-based) (recommended for new users) +- [System package managers](#system-package-managers) (simplest) +- [Building from source](#building-from-source) (for debugging and development) -Methods differ in ease of use, coverage, maintenance of old versions, -system-wide versus local environment use, and control. With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Environment-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In environment-based workflows, you install packages into an environment, which you +can activate and deactivate. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +If you have installed Python, you can create a virtual environment with a tool such +as `venv` - see [the tutorial in the Python Packaging User Guide][venv-guide]. +You can then install [SciPy from PyPI], the Python Package Index, with [`pip`]: -Other options include: + python -m pip install scipy -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +[venv-guide]: https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/tutorials/installing-packages/#creating-virtual-environments +[SciPy from PyPI]: https://pypi.org/project/scipy/ +[`pip`]: https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/getting-started/ - +Alternatively, you can install [SciPy from conda-forge] using a [Conda]-based +environment manager. You can install Python itself, as well as packages in +languages other than Python, as Conda packages.","path":"content/en/install.md","commit_id":"a4eda1e6eafd6b2c19b1eb17ab3111d8d2070c77","original_commit_id":"a4eda1e6eafd6b2c19b1eb17ab3111d8d2070c77","user":{"login":"stefanv","id":45071,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ1MDcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/45071?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv","html_url":"https://github.com/stefanv","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"```suggestion Alternatively, you can install [SciPy from conda-forge] using a [Conda]-based environment manager. With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Environment-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In environment-based workflows, you install packages into an environment, which you +can activate and deactivate. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +If you have installed Python, you can create a virtual environment with a tool such +as `venv` - see [the tutorial in the Python Packaging User Guide][venv-guide]. +You can then install [SciPy from PyPI], the Python Package Index, with [`pip`]: -Other options include: + python -m pip install scipy -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +[venv-guide]: https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/tutorials/installing-packages/#creating-virtual-environments +[SciPy from PyPI]: https://pypi.org/project/scipy/ +[`pip`]: https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/getting-started/ - +Alternatively, you can install [SciPy from conda-forge] using a [Conda]-based +environment manager. {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/pulls/comments/1890603853","pull_request_review_id":2512432349,"id":1890603853,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIq85wsFNN","diff_hunk":"@@ -3,101 +3,149 @@ title: Installation sidebar: false --- -Installations methods include: +The recommended method of installing SciPy depends on your preferred workflow. +The common workflows can roughly be broken down into the following +categories: -- [Distributions](#distributions) -- [pip](#pip-install) -- [conda](#pip-install) -- [Package Manager](#package_manager) -- [Source](#source) +- [Environment-based (e.g. `pip`, `conda`)](#environment-based) (the traditional workflow) +- [Project-based (e.g. `uv`, `pixi`)](#project-based) (recommended for new users) +- [System package managers](#system-package-managers) (simplest) +- [Building from source](#building-from-source) (for debugging and development) -Methods differ in ease of use, coverage, maintenance of old versions, -system-wide versus local environment use, and control. With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Environment-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In environment-based workflows, you install packages into an environment, which you +can activate and deactivate. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +If you have installed Python, you can create a virtual environment with a tool such +as `venv` - see [the tutorial in the Python Packaging User Guide][venv-guide]. +You can then install [SciPy from PyPI], the Python Package Index, with [`pip`]: -Other options include: + python -m pip install scipy -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +[venv-guide]: https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/tutorials/installing-packages/#creating-virtual-environments +[SciPy from PyPI]: https://pypi.org/project/scipy/ +[`pip`]: https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/getting-started/ - +Alternatively, you can install [SciPy from conda-forge] using a [Conda]-based +environment manager. You can install Python itself, as well as packages in +languages other than Python, as Conda packages. -# Installing with Pip +[Miniforge] is the recommended way to install `conda` and [`mamba`], +two Conda-based environment managers. You can then install SciPy as follows: -You can install SciPy from PyPI with `pip`: + conda install scipy # or + mamba install scipy - python -m pip install scipy +[SciPy from conda-forge]: https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/scipy +[Miniforge]: https://conda-forge.org/download/ +[Conda]: https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/index.html +[`mamba`]: https://mamba.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ + + + +## Project-based workflows + +In project-based workflows, a project is a directory containing a manifest +file describing the project, a lock-file describing the exact dependencies +of the project, and the project's (potentially multiple) environments. +These workflows provide reproducibility benefits over environment-based workflows. + +You can add [SciPy from PyPI] to a [`uv`] project: + + uv add scipy + +You can also install Python itself with `uv`. + +[`uv`]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/ + +If you work with non-Python packages, you may prefer to install SciPy as +a Conda package, so that you can use the same workflow for packages which +are not available on PyPI. - +You can add [SciPy from conda-forge] to a [`pixi`] project: -# Installing via Conda + pixi add scipy -You can install SciPy from the `defaults` or `conda-forge` channels with `conda`: +Pixi can also install packages from PyPI, using `uv` under the hood. - conda install scipy +[`pixi`]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ - + -# Install system-wide via a package manager +## Installing system-wide via a system package manager System package managers can install the most common Python packages. They install packages for the entire computer, often use older versions, -and don't have as many available versions. +and don't have as many available versions. {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/pulls/comments/1890606794","pull_request_review_id":2512432349,"id":1890606794,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIq85wsF7K","diff_hunk":"@@ -3,101 +3,149 @@ title: Installation sidebar: false --- -Installations methods include: +The recommended method of installing SciPy depends on your preferred workflow. +The common workflows can roughly be broken down into the following +categories: -- [Distributions](#distributions) -- [pip](#pip-install) -- [conda](#pip-install) -- [Package Manager](#package_manager) -- [Source](#source) +- [Environment-based (e.g. `pip`, `conda`)](#environment-based) (the traditional workflow) +- [Project-based (e.g. `uv`, `pixi`)](#project-based) (recommended for new users) +- [System package managers](#system-package-managers) (simplest) +- [Building from source](#building-from-source) (for debugging and development) -Methods differ in ease of use, coverage, maintenance of old versions, -system-wide versus local environment use, and control. With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Environment-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In environment-based workflows, you install packages into an environment, which you +can activate and deactivate. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +If you have installed Python, you can create a virtual environment with a tool such +as `venv` - see [the tutorial in the Python Packaging User Guide][venv-guide]. +You can then install [SciPy from PyPI], the Python Package Index, with [`pip`]: -Other options include: + python -m pip install scipy -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +[venv-guide]: https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/tutorials/installing-packages/#creating-virtual-environments +[SciPy from PyPI]: https://pypi.org/project/scipy/ +[`pip`]: https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/getting-started/ - +Alternatively, you can install [SciPy from conda-forge] using a [Conda]-based +environment manager. You can install Python itself, as well as packages in +languages other than Python, as Conda packages. -# Installing with Pip +[Miniforge] is the recommended way to install `conda` and [`mamba`], +two Conda-based environment managers. You can then install SciPy as follows: -You can install SciPy from PyPI with `pip`: + conda install scipy # or + mamba install scipy - python -m pip install scipy +[SciPy from conda-forge]: https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/scipy +[Miniforge]: https://conda-forge.org/download/ +[Conda]: https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/index.html +[`mamba`]: https://mamba.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ + + + +## Project-based workflows + +In project-based workflows, a project is a directory containing a manifest +file describing the project, a lock-file describing the exact dependencies +of the project, and the project's (potentially multiple) environments. +These workflows provide reproducibility benefits over environment-based workflows. + +You can add [SciPy from PyPI] to a [`uv`] project: + + uv add scipy + +You can also install Python itself with `uv`. + +[`uv`]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/ + +If you work with non-Python packages, you may prefer to install SciPy as +a Conda package, so that you can use the same workflow for packages which +are not available on PyPI. - +You can add [SciPy from conda-forge] to a [`pixi`] project: -# Installing via Conda + pixi add scipy -You can install SciPy from the `defaults` or `conda-forge` channels with `conda`: +Pixi can also install packages from PyPI, using `uv` under the hood. - conda install scipy +[`pixi`]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ - + -# Install system-wide via a package manager +## Installing system-wide via a system package manager System package managers can install the most common Python packages. They install packages for the entire computer, often use older versions, -and don't have as many available versions. +and don't have as many available versions. They are not the recommended +installation method, but they are the simplest. -## Ubuntu and Debian +### Ubuntu and Debian Using `apt-get`: sudo apt-get install python3-scipy -## Fedora +### Fedora Using `dnf`: sudo dnf install python3-scipy -## macOS +### macOS macOS doesn't have a preinstalled package manager, but you can install [Homebrew](https://brew.sh/) and use it to install SciPy (and Python itself): brew install scipy - + -# Source packages +## Building from source A word of warning: building SciPy from source can be a nontrivial exercise. We -recommend using binaries instead if those are available for your platform. +recommend using binaries instead if those are available for your platform +via one of the above methods. For details on how to build from source, see -[this guide in the SciPy docs](https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/building/index.html). +[the building from source guide in the SciPy docs][building-docs]. + +[building-docs]: https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/building/index.html + + + +## Installing with type stubs + +Static type stubs are available via a separate package, `scipy-stubs`, on +PyPI and conda-forge. If you have already installed SciPy, you just need to +install `scipy-stubs` in the same way: + + python -m pip install scipy-stubs # or + uv add scipy-stubs # or + conda install scipy-stubs # or + pixi add scipy-stubs + + + +You can also install SciPy and `scipy-stubs` as a single package, +via the `scipy-stubs[scipy]` extra on PyPI, or the `scipy-typed` +package on conda-forge. +To get a specific version `x.y.z` of SciPy (such as `1.14.1`), +you should install version `x.y.z.*` below: + + python -m pip install "scipy-stubs[scipy]" # or + # versions for illustrative purposes + uv add "scipy-stubs[scipy]==1.14.1.*" # or + conda install "scipy-typed>=1.14" # or + pixi add "scipy-typed=1.15.0.*"","path":"content/en/install.md","commit_id":"a4eda1e6eafd6b2c19b1eb17ab3111d8d2070c77","original_commit_id":"a4eda1e6eafd6b2c19b1eb17ab3111d8d2070c77","user":{"login":"stefanv","id":45071,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ1MDcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/45071?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv","html_url":"https://github.com/stefanv","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/stefanv/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"```suggestion ``` I suggest keeping this section short, to keep the overall document concise. With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Environment-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In environment-based workflows, you install packages into an environment, which you +can activate and deactivate. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +If you have installed Python, you can create a virtual environment with a tool such +as `venv` - see [the tutorial in the Python Packaging User Guide][venv-guide]. +You can then install [SciPy from PyPI], the Python Package Index, with [`pip`]: -Other options include: + python -m pip install scipy -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +[venv-guide]: https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/tutorials/installing-packages/#creating-virtual-environments +[SciPy from PyPI]: https://pypi.org/project/scipy/ +[`pip`]: https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/getting-started/ - +Alternatively, you can install [SciPy from conda-forge] using a [Conda]-based +environment manager. You can install Python itself, as well as packages in +languages other than Python, as Conda packages. -# Installing with Pip +[Miniforge] is the recommended way to install `conda` and [`mamba`], +two Conda-based environment managers. `validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. First time pull requesting to a public repo, let me know if anything is `validate` function in `validate.py` for empty docstring error `GL08` ("The object does not have a docstring"), specifically for `__init__` constructors. If an empty docstring for `__init__`, will first check if class docstring is satisfied for parameter mismatches, before deciding to report `GL08` (if mismatches exist, i.e. incomplete documentation).

Implemented new docstring test classes in `test_validate.py` to verify correct functionality.
Also updated readthedocs `format.rst` and `validation.rst` to reflect new changes to constructor checking behavior.

Solves https://github.com/numpy/numpydoc/issues/591.

Note, I think a modification to the readthedocs  https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/validation.html#ignoring-validation-checks-with-inline-comments ought to be made 

P.s. First time pull requesting to a public repo, let me know if anything is Description:

When trying to build the Debian package for version 0.25.0, I get the failures of **test_2d_bf**, **test_2d_cg**, **test_2d_cg_mg** and **test_2d_cg_j** with the **float16** parameter:
```

dtype = <class 'numpy.float16'>

    @testing.parametrize('dtype', [np.float16, np.float32, np.float64])
    def test_2d_bf(dtype):
        lx = 70
        ly = 100
    
        # have to use a smaller beta to avoid warning with lower precision input
        beta = 90 if dtype == np.float64 else 25
    
        data, labels = make_2d_syntheticdata(lx, ly)
        data = data.astype(dtype, copy=False)
>       labels_bf = random_walker(data, labels, beta=beta, mode='bf')

skimage/segmentation/tests/test_random_walker.py:73: 
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ 
skimage/_shared/utils.py:445: in fixed_func
    return func(*args, **kwargs)
skimage/segmentation/random_walker_segmentation.py:554: in random_walker
    lap_sparse, B = _build_linear_system(
skimage/segmentation/random_walker_segmentation.py:168: in _build_linear_system
    lap_sparse = _build_laplacian(
skimage/segmentation/random_walker_segmentation.py:148: in _build_laplacian
    lap = sparse.csr_array((data, (i_indices, j_indices)), shape=(pixel_nb, pixel_nb))
/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/scipy/sparse/_compressed.py:56: in __init__
    arrays = coo._coo_to_compressed(self._swap)
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ 

self = <COOrdinate sparse array of dtype 'float16'
        with 27660 stored elements and shape (7000, 7000)>
swap = <function _csr_base._swap at 0x7f737c6fbce0>, copy = False
    def _coo_to_compressed(self, swap, copy=False):
        """convert (shape, coords, data) to (indptr, indices, data, shape)"""
        M, N = swap(self._shape_as_2d)
        # convert idx_dtype intc to int32 for pythran.
        # tested in scipy/optimize/tests/test__numdiff.py::test_group_columns
        idx_dtype = self._get_index_dtype(self.coords, maxval=max(self.nnz, N))
    
        if self.ndim == 1:
            indices = self.coords[0].copy() if copy else self.coords[0]
            nnz = len(indices)
            indptr = np.array([0, nnz], dtype=idx_dtype)
            data = self.data.copy() if copy else self.data
            return indptr, indices, data, self.shape
    
        # ndim == 2
        major, minor = swap(self.coords)
        nnz = len(major)
        major = major.astype(idx_dtype, copy=False)
        minor = minor.astype(idx_dtype, copy=False)
    
        indptr = np.empty(M + 1, dtype=idx_dtype)
        indices = np.empty_like(minor, dtype=idx_dtype)
        data = np.empty_like(self.data, dtype=self.dtype)
    
>       coo_tocsr(M, N, nnz, major, minor, self.data, indptr, indices, data)
E       ValueError: Output dtype not compatible with inputs.

/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/scipy/sparse/_coo.py:374: ValueError
```

### Way to reproduce:

This happens when running the tests during the Debian build of the package.

### Version information:

```Shell
* Python 3.12.8 and Python 3.13.1
* Linux-6.11.10-amd64-x86_64-with-glibc2.40
* skimage 0.25.0
* numpy 1.26.4
* scipy 1.14.1
```

Description:

While trying to install `scikit-image` version 0.25.0 in a **Termux environment** on an aarch64-based system (ARM architecture), the installation process fails during the compilation step. Specifically, the issue seems to arise from the use of the `cpow` library function, which is undeclared.
I expect the package to install correctly without errors.
#### Error details:
The critical error occurs when compiling the file `skimage/measure/_marching_cubes_lewiner_cy.cpython-312.so.p/_marching_cubes_lewiner_cy.c`. The relevant part of the error message is as follows:
``` 
error: call to undeclared library function 'cpow' with type '_Complex double (_Complex double, _Complex double)'; ISO C99 and later do not support implicit function declarations [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
```
The error suggests including `<complex.h>` or explicitly declaring `cpow`, but this is part of the package's source code and cannot be resolved on the user's side.
I suspect this issue is related to:
- Missing support for `cpow` or `<complex.h>` in the environment.
- A potential compatibility issue with the Termux environment or the ARM architecture.

#### Environment:
- **Operating system:** Termux (Android-based)
- **Processor architecture:** aarch64 (ARM)
- **Python version:** Python 3.12
- **C compiler:** clang 19.1.5
- **Build tools:** meson 1.6.1, ninja 1.12.1
- **scikit-image version:** 0.25.0

#### Additional details:
Despite having all necessary dependencies (such as `numpy`, `scipy`, etc.) installed, the package does not install as expected. If a custom header file or patch is needed to support `cpow` on this environment/architecture, additional guidance would be appreciated.

With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Environment-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In environment-based workflows, you install packages into an environment, which you +can activate and deactivate. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +If you have installed Python, you can create a virtual environment with a tool such +as `venv` - see [the tutorial in the Python Packaging User Guide][venv-guide]. +You can then install [SciPy from PyPI], the Python Package Index, with [`pip`]: -Other options include: + python -m pip install scipy -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +[venv-guide]: https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/tutorials/installing-packages/#creating-virtual-environments +[SciPy from PyPI]: https://pypi.org/project/scipy/ +[`pip`]: https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/getting-started/ - +Alternatively, you can install [SciPy from conda-forge] using a [Conda]-based +environment manager. You can install Python itself, as well as packages in +languages other than Python, as Conda packages. -# Installing with Pip +[Miniforge] is the recommended way to install `conda` and [`mamba`], +two Conda-based environment managers. {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/pulls/comments/1890643394","pull_request_review_id":2512525840,"id":1890643394,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIq85wsO3C","diff_hunk":"@@ -3,101 +3,149 @@ title: Installation sidebar: false --- -Installations methods include: +The recommended method of installing SciPy depends on your preferred workflow. +The common workflows can roughly be broken down into the following +categories: -- [Distributions](#distributions) -- [pip](#pip-install) -- [conda](#pip-install) -- [Package Manager](#package_manager) -- [Source](#source) +- [Environment-based (e.g. `pip`, `conda`)](#environment-based) (the traditional workflow) +- [Project-based (e.g. `uv`, `pixi`)](#project-based) (recommended for new users) +- [System package managers](#system-package-managers) (simplest) +- [Building from source](#building-from-source) (for debugging and development) -Methods differ in ease of use, coverage, maintenance of old versions, -system-wide versus local environment use, and control. With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Environment-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In environment-based workflows, you install packages into an environment, which you +can activate and deactivate. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +If you have installed Python, you can create a virtual environment with a tool such +as `venv` - see [the tutorial in the Python Packaging User Guide][venv-guide]. +You can then install [SciPy from PyPI], the Python Package Index, with [`pip`]: -Other options include: + python -m pip install scipy -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +[venv-guide]: https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/tutorials/installing-packages/#creating-virtual-environments +[SciPy from PyPI]: https://pypi.org/project/scipy/ +[`pip`]: https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/getting-started/ - +Alternatively, you can install [SciPy from conda-forge] using a [Conda]-based +environment manager. You can install Python itself, as well as packages in +languages other than Python, as Conda packages. -# Installing with Pip +[Miniforge] is the recommended way to install `conda` and [`mamba`], +two Conda-based environment managers. You can then install SciPy as follows: -You can install SciPy from PyPI with `pip`: + conda install scipy # or + mamba install scipy - python -m pip install scipy +[SciPy from conda-forge]: https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/scipy +[Miniforge]: https://conda-forge.org/download/ +[Conda]: https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/index.html +[`mamba`]: https://mamba.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ + + + +## Project-based workflows + +In project-based workflows, a project is a directory containing a manifest +file describing the project, a lock-file describing the exact dependencies +of the project, and the project's (potentially multiple) environments. +These workflows provide reproducibility benefits over environment-based workflows. + +You can add [SciPy from PyPI] to a [`uv`] project: + + uv add scipy + +You can also install Python itself with `uv`. + +[`uv`]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/ + +If you work with non-Python packages, you may prefer to install SciPy as +a Conda package, so that you can use the same workflow for packages which +are not available on PyPI. - +You can add [SciPy from conda-forge] to a [`pixi`] project: -# Installing via Conda + pixi add scipy -You can install SciPy from the `defaults` or `conda-forge` channels with `conda`: +Pixi can also install packages from PyPI, using `uv` under the hood. - conda install scipy +[`pixi`]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ - + -# Install system-wide via a package manager +## Installing system-wide via a system package manager System package managers can install the most common Python packages. They install packages for the entire computer, often use older versions, -and don't have as many available versions. +and don't have as many available versions. They are not the recommended +installation method, but they are the
